message.content:
Conduct a COMPREHENSIVE, INDEPENDENT audit of why the "cellcog" deep-research pipeline failed. Read the full brief here and execute ALL of it yourself, end to end:

/tmp/claude-1000/-home-polaris-polaris-project/21e87760-8436-4090-870d-99ef2121882e/scratchpad/cellcog_postmortem_brief.txt

This is READ-ONLY investigation — do NOT edit any code (other efforts are running). Repos: /home/polaris/wt/flywheel (cellcog) and /home/polaris/wt/outline_agent (champion). Investigate all 7 areas in the brief independently and rigorously — verify everything from the actual artifacts (read flywheel/outputs/release/report.md, outline_agent/docs/step3_insight_lever_ab/control_report.md, the score result files, the code, the git log / progress notes). Do NOT defer to any prior conclusion; form your own.

You have a particular edge here: the CORE failure is about WRITING QUALITY. Read both reports as a discerning reader/editor would — characterize precisely how cellcog's prose reads (mechanical? repetitive? robotic?) versus the champion's, quantify the repetition, and explain against the RACE criteria why cellcog scores ~0.23 on insight and ~0.22 on readability while the champion scores ~0.43 on both. That prose analysis is where you should be sharpest.

When done, WRITE your full audit to this file (so it can be consolidated with the other independent audit):
/tmp/claude-1000/-home-polaris-polaris-project/21e87760-8436-4090-870d-99ef2121882e/scratchpad/fable_postmortem_ANSWER.md

Your final message back to me should be a concise summary: your single-sentence ROOT CAUSE verdict + the 3-5 most important findings. Be thorough in the file, concise in the reply.

attachment.content:
- deep-research: Deep research harness — fan-out web searches, fetch sources, adversarially verify claims, synthesize a cited report. - When the user wants a deep, multi-source, fact-checked research report on any topic. BEFORE invoking, check if the question is specific enough to research directly — if underspecified (e.g., "what car to buy" without budget/use-case/region), ask 2-3 clarifying questions to narrow scope. Then pass the refined question as args, weaving the answers in.
- dataviz: Use this skill whenever you are about to create ANY chart, graph, plot, dashboard, or data visualization, in ANY output medium — an HTML or React artifact, inline SVG, plotting code in any library (matplotlib, plotly, d3, Recharts, …), an image/PNG you will render and upload, or a chart shared into Slack. Read it BEFORE writing the first line of chart code, choosing chart colors, building a stat tile / meter / KPI row, or laying out a dashboard. Produces visualizations that read as one system — elegant, accessible, consistent in light and dark — using a brand-neutral placeholder palette you swap for your own. Teaches a design-system-agnostic method: a form heuristic, a color formula with a runnable validator, mark specs, and interaction rules. A validated default palette is documented in `references/palette.md` — swap that file's values for your brand's. Triggers on: "chart", "graph", "plot", "data viz", "visualization", "dashboard", "analytics", "visualize data", "categorical colors", "sequential / diverging palette", "stat tile", "sparkline", "heatmap", "legend", "axis", "tooltip", "chart colors", "color by series".
- artifact-design: Design guidance and fundamentals for Artifacts.
- update-config: Use this skill to configure the Claude Code harness via settings.json. Automated behaviors ("from now on when X", "each time X", "whenever X", "before/after X") require hooks configured in settings.json - the harness executes these, not Claude, so memory/preferences cannot fulfill them. Also use for: permissions ("allow X", "add permission", "move permission to"), env vars ("set X=Y"), hook troubleshooting, or any changes to settings.json/settings.local.json files. Examples: "allow npm commands", "add bq permission to global settings", "move permission to user settings", "set DEBUG=true", "when claude stops show X". For simple settings like theme/model, suggest the /config command.
- keybindings-help: Use when the user wants to customize keyboard shortcuts, rebind keys, add chord bindings, or modify ~/.claude/keybindings.json. Examples: "rebind ctrl+s", "add a chord shortcut", "change the submit key", "customize keybindings".
- verify: Verify that a code change actually does what it's supposed to by exercising it end-to-end and observing behavior — drive the affected flow, not just tests or typecheck. Run before committing nontrivial changes; bootstraps this repo's project verify skill if none exists yet. Don't invoke it on a diff that only touches tests, docs, or other code with no runtime surface to drive (a change to product source always has one) — there's nothing to observe.
- code-review: Review the current diff for correctness bugs and reuse/simplification/efficiency cleanups at the given effort level (low/medium: fewer, high-confidence findings; high→max: broader coverage, may include uncertain findings; ultra: deep multi-agent review in the cloud). Pass --comment to post findings as inline PR comments, or --fix to apply the findings to the working tree after the review.
- simplify: Review the changed code for reuse, simplification, efficiency, and altitude cleanups, then apply the fixes. Quality only — it does not hunt for bugs; use /code-review for that.
- fewer-permission-prompts: Scan your transcripts for common read-only Bash and MCP tool calls, then add a prioritized allowlist to project .claude/settings.json to reduce permission prompts.
- loop: Run a prompt or slash command on a recurring interval (e.g. /loop 5m /foo). Omit the interval to let the model self-pace. - When the user wants to set up a recurring task, poll for status, or run something repeatedly on an interval (e.g. "check the deploy every 5 minutes", "keep running /babysit-prs"). Do NOT invoke for one-off tasks.
- schedule: Create, update, list, or run scheduled cloud agents (routines) that execute on a cron schedule. - When the user wants to schedule a recurring cloud agent, set up automated tasks, create a cron job for Claude Code, or manage their scheduled agents/routines. Also use when the user wants a one-time scheduled run ("run this once at 3pm", "remind me to check X tomorrow").
- claude-api: Reference for the Claude API / Anthropic SDK — model ids, pricing, params, streaming, tool use, MCP, agents, caching, token counting, model migration.
TRIGGER — read BEFORE opening the target file; don't skip because it "looks like a one-liner" — whenever: the prompt names Claude/Anthropic in any form (Claude, Anthropic, Fable, Opus, Sonnet, Haiku, `anthropic`, `@anthropic-ai`, `claude-*`, `us.anthropic.*`, `[1m]`); the user asks about an LLM (pricing/model choice/limits/caching) — never answer from memory; OR the task is LLM-shaped with provider unstated (agent/MCP/tool-definition/multi-agent/RAG/LLM-judge/computer-use; generate/summarize/extract/classify/rewrite/converse over NL; debugging refusals/cutoffs/streaming/tool-calls/tokens).
SKIP only when another provider is being worked on (overrides all triggers): OpenAI/GPT/Gemini/Llama/Mistral/Cohere/Ollama named in the query; OR `grep -rE 'openai|langchain_openai|google.generativeai|genai|mistralai|cohere|ollama'` over the project hits (run this grep FIRST if no provider named — don't Read the file).
- run: Launch and drive this project's app to see a change working. Use when asked to run, start, or screenshot the app, or to confirm a change works in the real app (not just tests). First looks for a project skill that already covers launching the app; otherwise falls back to built-in patterns per project type (CLI, server, TUI, Electron, browser-driven, library).
- init: Initialize a new CLAUDE.md file with codebase documentation
- review: Review a GitHub pull request; for your working diff use /code-review
- security-review: Complete a security review of the pending changes on the current branch

content.content:
1	COMPREHENSIVE INDEPENDENT AUDIT: Why did the "cellcog" deep-research pipeline fail? Investigate EVERYTHING below on your own, end to end. Another investigator is auditing the same question independently; an opus process will consolidate both — so be THOROUGH and INDEPENDENT, do not assume anything is out of scope. READ-ONLY (do not edit code; a separate effort is running). Repos: /home/polaris/wt/flywheel (cellcog) and /home/polaris/wt/outline_agent (the "champion" pipeline, for comparison).
2	
3	## THE FACTS (verified)
4	- GOAL: beat DeepResearch Bench RACE SOTA = 0.5603 on task 72 (a literature review on AI's restructuring of the labor market). RACE Overall = target/(target+reference); dimensions & rough weights: Comprehensiveness 0.29, Insight 0.32, Instruction-Following 0.21, Readability 0.15 (weights vary per task). Criteria live in outline_agent/third_party/deep_research_bench/prompt/criteria_prompt_en.py and score_prompt_en.py.
5	- We spent an entire overnight on "cellcog" (the compose pipeline: /home/polaris/wt/flywheel/scripts/cellcog_composer.py + report_ast.py + synthesis_contract.py + publisher.py). It was our highest hope. It FAILED.
6	- Cellcog scores, task 72:
7	  - First publish (thin, 1,881 words): Overall 0.2083 (Comp 0.1998, Insight 0.1861, IF 0.2306, Read 0.2353).
8	  - After the FULL fix (uncapped, relaxed gates, 7,703 words, 820 cards, 45+ cross-source verdicts): Overall 0.2691 (Comp 0.3167, Insight 0.2336, IF 0.2847, Read 0.2153). Result file: flywheel/third_party/deep_research_bench/results/race/cellcog_7703/race_result.txt. Report: flywheel/outputs/release/report.md.
9	- The CHAMPION pipeline (outline_agent, a STORM/multi-section generator) scores Overall 0.4447 (Comp 0.4569, Insight 0.4293, IF 0.4587, Read 0.4310) on task 72 with only ~3,914 words / ~88 works. Report: outline_agent/docs/step3_insight_lever_ab/control_report.md ; score: results/race/polaris_step3_control/race_result.txt.
10	- So cellcog with 820 cards / 7,703 words scored ~0.27 while the champion with fewer cards / half the words scored ~0.44 — nearly 2x on EVERY dimension.
11	
12	## INVESTIGATE ALL OF THIS (independently, thoroughly)
13	1. PROSE / WRITING QUALITY (likely the core failure). Read flywheel/outputs/release/report.md in depth. Characterize HOW cellcog writes vs how the champion writes (read outline_agent/docs/step3_insight_lever_ab/control_report.md). Cellcog appears mechanical/repetitive: many sentences are "The evidence [Author] present in [Journal] in [Year] is that...", and ~1 in 5 sentences is level-disclosure meta-commentary ("The evidence here is measured at the worker level; what follows is measured at the industry level"). Quantify the repetition. Explain WHY this tanks Insight (0.23) and Readability (0.22) against the RACE criteria. Is this style FUNDAMENTAL to cellcog's design or fixable?
14	2. ARCHITECTURE. Why does cellcog's design produce this prose? It is faithfulness-first: every sentence must bind to ONE card span (attribution), cross-source claims must be typed "verdicts" over admitted premises, and indirect-unit comparisons must DISCLOSE their level (the "measured at X level" scaffolding). Trace how these design choices (report_ast.py sentence-node model, synthesis_contract.py typed verdicts + level disclosure, the per-card attribution renderer) FORCE mechanical prose. Contrast with the champion's free-writing + strict_verify (outline_agent/src/polaris_graph/generator/) which writes flowing prose then verifies.
15	3. THE STRATEGIC DRIFT. The overnight effort drifted into building an ADVERSARY-PROOF faithfulness gate ("1,295 attacks / 0 bypasses" binding gate) — a NON-GOAL. The goal was insight/comprehensiveness/readability. Reconstruct (from git log, progress notes FLYWHEEL_PROGRESS.md / OVERNIGHT_PROGRESS.md, code comments) HOW/WHY the effort drifted from "score high" to "prove faithfulness", and what that cost. When did cellcog diverge from the champion lineage?
16	4. THE TECHNICAL FAILURE CASCADE. Enumerate every failure that ate the night and WHY each happened and hid the next: glm-5.2/OpenRouter SSE keep-alive hangs; a hardcoded [:12] findings cap; sentence-splitter crashes on period-bearing tokens (venue "Cureus.", author "Md."/"DB.", and the bold label "**Methods.**"); the HEADING_IS_A_PROPOSITION publish block from removing the publish fail-open; empty subsections from SSE gutting. Why did these STACK and mask each other, so we never saw the real problem (prose quality) until the very end?
17	5. THE SCORE, DIMENSION BY DIMENSION. Map each low dimension (Comp 0.32, Insight 0.23, IF 0.28, Read 0.22) to specific cellcog design choices. Why is Comprehensiveness the best-but-still-low despite 820 cards? Why is Instruction-Following low (does the report actually satisfy the task's implicit requirements)? Read the RACE criteria to ground each.
18	6. SALVAGEABILITY. What of the cellcog effort is worth keeping? (the 5,900-card corpus/mine? the faithfulness verification as a POST-hoc check on the champion's output? the cross-source verdict IDEA if written naturally?) What is a dead end (the compose/render/attribution architecture)? Should the corpus feed the champion instead (that is now being attempted)?
19	7. LESSONS. What should have been done differently? What does this teach about (a) what the RACE judge actually rewards, (b) faithfulness vs readability tradeoffs, (c) not iterating on the wrong pipeline for a whole night without a comparison baseline?
20	
21	## DELIVER (your independent audit)
22	A thorough written audit answering ALL 7 with concrete evidence: file:line references, quoted report excerpts (cellcog vs champion), quantified repetition stats, the score data, and the git/progress-note trail for the strategic drift. State your single-sentence ROOT CAUSE verdict, then the supporting analysis. Be independent and rigorous — do not defer to any prior conclusion; verify from the artifacts yourself.
23

Result file: flywheel/third_party/deep_research_bench/results/race/cellcog_7703/race_result.txt. Report: flywheel/outputs/release/report.md.
- The CHAMPION pipeline (outline_agent, a STORM/multi-section generator) scores Overall 0.4447 (Comp 0.4569, Insight 0.4293, IF 0.4587, Read 0.4310) on task 72 with only ~3,914 words / ~88 works. - So cellcog with 820 cards / 7,703 words scored ~0.27 while the champion with fewer cards / half the words scored ~0.44 — nearly 2x on EVERY dimension.

## INVESTIGATE ALL OF THIS (independently, thoroughly)
1. PROSE / WRITING QUALITY (likely the core failure). Read flywheel/outputs/release/report.md in depth. Characterize HOW cellcog writes vs how the champion writes (read outline_agent/docs/step3_insight_lever_ab/control_report.md). Cellcog appears mechanical/repetitive: many sentences are "The evidence [Author] present in [Journal] in [Year] is that...", and ~1 in 5 sentences is level-disclosure meta-commentary ("The evidence here is measured at the worker level; what follows is measured at the industry level"). Quantify the repetition. Explain WHY this tanks Insight (0.23) and Readability (0.22) against the RACE criteria. Is this style FUNDAMENTAL to cellcog's design or fixable?
2. ARCHITECTURE. Why does cellcog's design produce this prose? It is faithfulness-first: every sentence must bind to ONE card span (attribution), cross-source claims must be typed "verdicts" over admitted premises, and indirect-unit comparisons must DISCLOSE their level (the "measured at X level" scaffolding). Trace how these design choices (report_ast.py sentence-node model, synthesis_contract.py typed verdicts + level disclosure, the per-card attribution renderer) FORCE mechanical prose. Contrast with the champion's free-writing + strict_verify (outline_agent/src/polaris_graph/generator/) which writes flowing prose then verifies.
3. THE STRATEGIC DRIFT. The overnight effort drifted into building an ADVERSARY-PROOF faithfulness gate ("1,295 attacks / 0 bypasses" binding gate) — a NON-GOAL. The goal was insight/comprehensiveness/readability. Reconstruct (from git log, progress notes FLYWHEEL_PROGRESS.md / OVERNIGHT_PROGRESS.md, code comments) HOW/WHY the effort drifted from "score high" to "prove faithfulness", and what that cost. When did cellcog diverge from the champion lineage?
4. THE TECHNICAL FAILURE CASCADE. Enumerate every failure that ate the night and WHY each happened and hid the next: glm-5.2/OpenRouter SSE keep-alive hangs; a hardcoded [:12] findings cap; sentence-splitter crashes on period-bearing tokens (venue "Cureus.", author "Md."/"DB.", and the bold label "**Methods.**"); the HEADING_IS_A_PROPOSITION publish block from removing the publish fail-open; empty subsections from SSE gutting. Why did these STACK and mask each other, so we never saw the real problem (prose quality) until the very end?
5. THE SCORE, DIMENSION BY DIMENSION. Map each low dimension (Comp 0.32, Insight 0.23, IF 0.28, Read 0.22) to specific cellcog design choices. content.content:
1	# Artificial Intelligence and the Restructuring of the Labor Market: A Literature Review of Fourth Industrial Revolution Disruptions Across Industries
2	
3	## Abstract
4	
5	**Objective.** This review examines the evidence on restructuring impact of AI on the labor market.
6	
7	**Methods.** This review draws exclusively on peer-reviewed journal articles; it applies a high-quality standard to every source admitted; it admits only articles published in English; it excludes books, conference proceedings, preprints, working papers, news articles, which fall outside the requested source class. Sources that could not be verified against the stated criteria were excluded rather than reported with a caveat. Every finding below is taken from a verbatim passage of the source it is credited to, and no finding is stated that its cited source does not itself state.
8	
9	## Scope, Methods, and Source Selection
10	
11	**Source policy.** This review draws exclusively on peer-reviewed journal articles; it applies a high-quality standard to every source admitted; it admits only articles published in English; it excludes books, conference proceedings, preprints, working papers, news articles, which fall outside the requested source class.
12	
13	**Evidence base.** The findings below rest on eight hundred and twenty verified passages drawn from two hundred and seventy-nine permitted sources. A passage is admitted only where the source's own text states the finding attributed to it.
14	
15	## AI as the Driver of the Fourth Industrial Revolution
16	
17	### Why AI is a general-purpose technology rather than a single innovation
18	
19	The simultaneity of widespread labor-market anxiety and the very recent surge in scholarly attention establishes that AI's general-purpose character is still being recognized in real time, and the evidence does not yet settle whether perceived threat will translate into measurable displacement or into complementary task reconfiguration across disparate domains.
20	
21	### The scale and speed of the disruption claimed in the literature
22	
23	The evidence here is measured at the worker level. Writing in PLoS ONE in 2026, Clara Punzi and colleagues show that platform workers perform delivering products or packages (16%), delivering meals (27%), performing online tasks such as data entry and software development (29% overall), domestic work (8%), driving someone by car (10%), and other unspecified activities (9%). Ekaterina Zubkova, writing in the Journal of Digital Art & Humanities in 2026, report that the differences between algorithmic and human assessment cannot be explained solely by the amount of available information but arise from fundamentally different approaches to interpreting candidate experience.
24	
25	The evidence to this point is measured at the worker level; what follows is measured at the industry level. A 2025 Information Systems Frontiers study by Trinh Nguyen and Amany Elbanna finds that the total number of articles was two in 2018 and remained stable until 2020, following which there was a significant increase from 2020 to 2022. Goos and colleagues establish, in the American Economic Review in 2014, that estimating equation gives a point estimate of 0.42 with a standard error of 0.07. The acknowledged risk that reliance on large-scale, pre-existing language data may introduce bias, sometimes leading to unnatural or erroneous output, sets a boundary on displacement projections: the surge in research attention has not yet produced evidence that intelligent systems can handle nuanced, domain-specific labor contexts without systematic error, limiting the generalizability of claims about the speed of occupational disruption.
26	
27	**Table 1. Quantitative findings across the reviewed literature.** Each row reports a figure stated by the cited paper itself, verified against that paper's own bytes.
28	
29	| Study | Journal | Quantitative finding |
30	|---|---|---|
31	| Roberta Capello and Camilla Lenzi, 2023 | npj Urban Sustainability | -0.109 to -0.160 in employment to population ratio per unit robot density growth (top 25%, top 20%, top 10% cities and non-cities) [(0.041) to (0.059); p < 0.01, p < 0.05] |
32	| Daniel Freeman and colleagues, 2019 | Psychological Medicine | inadequate model fit (χ2 = 2599.2, df = 463, CFI = 0.91, TLI = 0.90, RMSEA = 0.087, SRMR = 0.038) in model fit of the original two-factor structure of Part A and B (1212 participants with complete data for the full GPTS) |
33	| Jasmine Balloch and colleagues, 2024 | Future Healthcare Journal | 100% versus 66%, and 94% versus 71% in clinician ability to provide full attention and positive experience in clinic per AI tool versus EHR alone (clinicians; healthcare) |
34	| Mauro Cazzaniga and colleagues, 2025 | Economía | 2.4 percent, 2.2 percent, 3 percent, 2.6 percent in expected lifetime earnings drop (non-college-educated individuals, those with a degree; UK, Brazil) |
35	| Joel Jia Wei Ng and colleagues, 2025 | BMC Medical Informatics and Decisi | 18.11% longer task completion times; 390 errors vs 245 errors in task completion time and error count for clinical documentation compared to using Keyboard and Mouse (KBM) (clinical documentation) |
36	| Romana Emilia Cramarenco and colleagues, 2023 | Oeconomia Copernicana | 11 articles in focus on the need for reskilling as an adaptive behavior of employees related to the advancement of technologies and AI adoption in the workplace per out of 15 selected articles (selected articles) |
37	
38	## Theoretical Frameworks for Technological Displacement
39	
40	### Skill-biased technical change and its limits
41	
42	The evidence Zivko Krstic present in the Croatian Regional Development Journal in 2024 is that skill-biased technological change, as propelled by advancements in AI, distinctly highlights the divergence in labour market dynamics between high-skill and low-skill jobs, while He Ying find, in their 2026 Journal of Artificial Intelligence and Information paper, that the skill-fission perspective identifies the technological empowerment of high-skilled groups alongside heightened risk of job substitution facing middle- and low-skilled groups and a temporal lag between educational response and technological change. Writing in the International Journal of Research in E-learning in 2024, Nian-Shing Chen and colleagues show that AI technologies can significantly enhance the support provided to educators, automating administrative tasks, grading, and basic tutoring, allowing educators to focus on more complex teaching challenges or offer support to a larger number of students.
43	
44	The evidence above is drawn at the worker level; the evidence below is drawn at the industry level. Anitha Selvaraj, writing in the Bodhi International Journal of Research in Humanities Arts and Science in 2025, report that the 4.2% decline in manufacturing employment is correlated with AI penetration, while youth NEET rates of 29.2% indicate risk from skill mismatches, while in the Journal of Economic Perspectives in 2019, Acemoglu and Restrepo show that between 1947 and 1987 the displacement effect reduced labor demand at about 0.48 percent per year but simultaneously there was an equally strong reinstatement effect equivalent to an increase in labor demand of 0.47 percent per year. A 2025 Applied and Computational Engineering study by Zihan Dong finds that these values correspond to about 0.18–0.49 standard deviations of change in the skill structure. Schwabe and Castellacci establish, in PLOS ONE in 2020, that the marginal effect for workers without a college degree is negative and significant, indicating that fear of replacement increases the probability that low-educated workers will report high job satisfaction by nearly 50%, while the corresponding marginal effect for workers with a University degree is not statistically significant. The evidence Sigal Alon present in Work and Occupations in 2022 is that the ratio between supply (job seekers) and demand (vacancies) was 7.1 (seven job seekers for any single job opening) in the third quarter of 2020, compared to 3.5 on the eve of the pandemic, with job seekers exceeding vacancies in all occupations.
45	
46	### The task-based framework: displacement, reinstatement, and the labor share
47	
48	The evidence here is measured at the industry level. Writing in the Journal of Economic Perspectives in 2019, Acemoglu and Restrepo show that between 1947 and 1987, the displacement effect reduced labor demand at about 0.48 percent per year, but simultaneously, there was an equally strong reinstatement effect, equivalent to an increase in labor demand of 0.47 percent per year, while Mauro Cazzaniga and colleagues find, in their 2025 Economía paper, that in this combined scenario the expected lifetime earnings drop by 2.4 percent for non-college-educated individuals and by 2.2 percent for those with a degree in the UK, while in Brazil the decrease in earnings is 3 percent and 2.6 percent for each group, respectively. Ali Akbar Septiandri and colleagues, writing in PNAS nexus in 2024, report that the most-impacted occupation, Cardiovascular Technologists and Technicians, carries an AII of 0.64, with 16 of 25 tasks impacted and a task patent similarity of 0.8753, while Tolan and colleagues, writing in the Journal of Artificial Intelligence Research in 2021, report that correlations and Cronbach's Alpha values of between 0.8 and 0.9 suggest consistency in the measurement of task intensity across different data sources, yet it is reasonable to doubt the comprehensiveness of the task framework. The task-based framework's operational strength lies in patent-similarity scoring that identifies specific occupational exposures, but the measurement apparatus validating these scores concedes its own incompleteness, leaving displacement estimates contingent on a task taxonomy whose boundaries remain uncertain.
49	
50	The evidence to this point is measured at the industry level; what follows is measured at the worker level. A 2024 Future Healthcare Journal study by Jasmine Balloch and colleagues finds that 100% of clinicians reported being able to provide patients with their full attention whilst using the AI tool versus 66% using the EHR alone, and 94% agreed that they had a positive experience in clinic versus 71% using the EHR alone, while in the Journal of Human Resource and Leadership in 2026, Shamma Rashed show that 100% of the participants identified the need for training, workshops, mentoring, technical assistance and on-going learning.
51	
52	### Prediction machines: AI as a fall in the cost of prediction
53	
54	The evidence here is measured at the firm level. Enaam Ouda and Mahmoud Haggag establish, in Frontiers in Built Environment in 2025, that the annual savings at a 70% demand equal USD 155,511.30, found by subtracting the automated setup annual cost of USD 317,887.50 from the manual setup annual cost of USD 473,398.80, while Enaam Ouda and Mahmoud Haggag find, in their 2025 Frontiers in Built Environment paper, that while adopting automation requires a high investment in robotic systems, approximately 321% higher than the cost of manual setup, with an estimated payback period of around 3 years.
55	
56	The evidence to this point is measured at the firm level; what follows is measured at the worker level. The evidence Mustafa Ergün present in İnsan ve Toplum Bilimleri Araştırmaları Dergisi in 2025 is that the findings reveal persistent structural unemployment, particularly among women, and a stubbornly high rate of informal employment, which declined only moderately from 27.6% in 2019 to 19.2% in 2023, while Daniel Ainooson - Noonoo and David Nanor, writing in the Journal of Educational Technology and Online Learning in 2025, report that while only 16.0% further suggested that banning or discouraging the use of ChatGPT for official duties is necessary. Writing in the Cureus Journal of Business and Economics in 2025, Hong Chau Linh Nguyen and colleagues show that although GAI offers opportunities for productivity gains and safety improvements, its integration raises concerns about job displacement, skill gaps, and ethical dilemmas, while in the International Journal of Innovative Science and Research Technology (IJISRT) in 2026, Md Jahangir Sadat and A S M Sarfaraz Nawaz show that the largest effect was observed between Digital Literacy and Self-Efficacy (f² = 0.721), indicating digital competence is a major determinant of employees' confidence in AI-enabled workplace environments.
57	
58	The evidence to this point is measured at the worker level; what follows is measured at the economy level. Maha Kalai and colleagues, writing in the Journal of Economic Structures in 2024, report that in a long-term PMG-ARDL estimation, the AIit variable carries a coefficient of 0.217 with a standard deviation of 0.044, a t-Statistic of 4.98, and a probability of 0.000, while Sin-Yu Ho and Fiyinfoluwa Giwa find, in their 2026 Journal of the Knowledge Economy paper, that while a systematic review of 194 studies selected from an initial pool of 1,194 publications demonstrates that AI is increasingly shaping multiple dimensions of development, including labour markets and productivity. The observation that theoretical frameworks of public policy are essential but practically constrained implies that systematic reviews linking AI to multiple dimensions of development may overstate the determinism of the policy cycle in governing labor market restructuring. A 2023 Philosophy & Technology study by Markus Furendal and Karim Jebari finds that the discussion on the future of work must recognize a further distinction between human augmentation technologies, which promote workers’ pursuit of objective goods through work, and human stunting technologies, which increase productivity in ways that make the job worse for individual workers, while Rohit Madan and Mona Ashok, writing in the Government Information Quarterly in 2023, report that while 45% of the studies discuss AI in terms of machine learning, big data analytics, algorithmic decision making, automated decision making, and 5% of the studies. The occupational-level displacement evidence considered above bears directly on this question: although the framework distinguishing augmentation from stunting suggests productivity gains can harm workers, the measured distribution of exposure across occupations cannot distinguish which of these two mechanisms dominates. Arif Jetha and colleagues establish, in the Scandinavian Journal of Work Environment & Health in 2026, that the mean occupational LLM exposure value was 0.34 (minimum=0; maximum=0.84) across the four LFS survey years, while in Human Technology in 2024, Dominika Kaczorowska-Spychalska and colleagues show that while at this stage of technological development, human-AI collaboration in the knowledge management process is crucial, as the role of human beings in an accurate decision-making process is still significant.
59	
60	The evidence above is review evidence; the evidence below is survey evidence. The evidence Hanna Yarovenko and colleagues present in Human Technology in 2024 is that respondents are not convinced of the possibility of a “machine uprising,” which indicates a pragmatic approach to the future of AI (0.5526), and believe that AI will probably not be able to surpass human creativity or intelligence because they are unique (0, while Mascha Kurpicz-Briki and colleagues find, in their 2026 Frontiers in Artificial Intelligence paper, that while an additional 59 items were labeled “controversial,” reﬂecting disagreement on their bias orientation, and 58 items were rephrased to search for bias-mitigating terms. Writing in Cell Reports Medicine in 2022, Enrico Coiera and Sidong Liu show that the US NIH’s LitCovid hub had curated about 270,000 scientific articles from 8,000 journals by July 2022, while Daniel Freeman and colleagues, writing in Psychological Medicine in 2019, report that while the initial CFA in the 1212 participants with complete data for the full GPTS demonstrated the original two-factor structure of Part A and B had an inadequate model fit (χ2 = 2599.2, df = 463, CFI = 0.91, TLI = 0.90, RMSEA = 0.087, SRMR = 0.038). Julianne Holt-Lunstad and colleagues, writing in PLoS Medicine in 2010, report that significant differences were found across the type of social measurement evaluated (p,0.001), with the association strongest for complex measures of social integration (OR = 1.91; 95% CI 1.63 to 2.23) and lowest for binary indicators of residential status (OR = 1.19; 95% CI 0.99 to 1.44), while in PLOS Medicine in 2018, Jonathan Pearson-Stuttard and colleagues show that while all the scenarios were estimated with more than 80% probability to be cost-effective (incremental cost/QALY $100,000) by 2021 and to become cost-saving by 2031. A 2018 PLOS Medicine study by Jonathan Pearson-Stuttard and colleagues finds that between 2017 and 2036, the optimal reformulation scenario achieving the FDA sodium reduction targets could prevent approximately 450,000 CVD cases (95% uncertainty interval: 240,000 to 740,000), gain approximately 2.1 million discounted QALYs (1.7 million to 2.4 million), and produce discounted cost savings of approximately $41 billion ($14 billion to $81 billion), while Christian Tarsney find, in their 2017 Economics and Philosophy paper, that while there is simply no reason to think that representing considerations like the diminishing marginal utility of consumption, aversion to inequality, and concerns about over-demandingness by means of a social discount rate can meet this requirement in the context of climate policy. Christoph Engels and colleagues establish, in Artificial Intelligence and Law in 2024, that if a judge were to decide to jail the defendant whenever the score is equal or above 4, 577 (40.1%) defendants would be wrongly classified as too likely to re-offend to be left unsupervised, while only 73 (5.1%) would be wrongly classified as unlikely to re-offend, while Ayling and Chapman, writing in AI and Ethics in 2022, report that while a data set of n = 39 documents offered practical tools to operationalise ethical principles in the production and deployment of AI systems, excluding all that did not contain practical tools to apply ethical principles.
61	
62	## Measuring Exposure, Adoption, and Realized Outcomes
63	
64	### Exposure measures encode different theories of susceptibility
65	
66	The evidence here is measured at the occupation level. The evidence Arif Jetha and colleagues present in the Scandinavian Journal of Work Environment & Health in 2026 is that occupations with exposure to precarity had a significantly higher mean LLM exposure [mean 0.386, 95% confidence interval (CI) 0.356–0.417] compared to occupations with medium (mean 0.258, 95% CI 0.221–0.295), high (mean 0.260, 95% CI 0.194–0.328) or very high precarity (mean 0.205, 95% CI 0.136–0.275), while in Hipertext net Revista Académica sobre Documentación Digital y Comunicación Interactiva in 2026, Olga Samokhina and Javier Díaz-Noci show that although it is hardly possible now to fully displace highly qualified experts, the disruptive impact of AI cannot be ignored, and the necessity of human intervention is dramati- cally decreasing.
67	
68	The evidence above is drawn at the occupation level; the evidence below is drawn at the worker level. Writing in Behavioral Sciences in 2025, Mustafa Akben and Su Dong show that at high levels of job complexity (+1 SD), the indirect effect was negative and significant in Study 2 (B = −0.311, 95% CI [−0.608, −0.036]) as the negative effect on AI self-efficacy translates into reduced AI adoption behavior through decreased AI readiness, while Jainendra Kumar Verma and Kamal Krishna find, in their 2026 International Journal of Foreign Trade and International Business paper, that the experimental evidence included is concentrated in writing, customer support, consulting, and entrepreneurship tasks, so the generalizability of measured productivity effects to other occupations remains uncertain.
69	
70	The evidence to this point is measured at the worker level; what follows is measured at the occupation level. Olga Samokhina and Javier Díaz-Noci, writing in Hipertext net Revista Académica sobre Documentación Digital y Comunicación Interactiva in 2026, report that across the negative emotions, there are more posts about AI replacing tasks (slightly negative) than about AI replacing jobs (negative), while Piotr Sliż and Jędrzej Siciński, writing in Kwartalnik Nauk o Przedsiębiorstwie in 2026, report that this pattern becomes particularly pronounced following the widespread adoption of generative AI tools (post-ChatGPT period, 2022–2023), when both the volume of discussion and the polarization of sentiment significantly increased.
71	
72	The evidence above is drawn at the occupation level; the evidence below is drawn at the firm level. A 2025 Journal of Human Resource Management study by Ciro Alexander Gonçalves da SILVA finds that organisations applying anticipatory, data-driven reskilling strategies achieve higher adaptability and innovation outcomes, whereas reactive measures remain insufficient to close skill gaps in the long term, and in Entrepreneurship Theory and Practice in 2021, Chalmers and colleagues show that new tasks and job roles will service this new engine of the firm, leading to higher paid jobs that are increasingly outsourced to skilled self- employed agents. The findings jointly establish that anticipatory reskilling and structural firm reorganization are mutually reinforcing responses to automation, but they do not address whether such proactive measures can fully absorb the labor displaced by the contraction of legacy job categories.
73	
74	The aggregate regional exposure modeling implies that macroeconomic susceptibility is bound by structural labor-market variables, yet this methodological focus leaves a gap at the micro level that can only be bounded if organizations accurately measure their readiness prior to adoption.
75	
76	### Exposure is not adoption, and adoption is not impact
77	
78	These two findings jointly establish that occupational exposure scores capture the theoretical susceptibility of tasks to automation, whereas actual adoption data reveal a workforce whose capacity and role structure mediate whether that susceptibility translates into deployment, thereby underscoring that exposure metrics systematically overstate the population reached by the technology. Jöhnk and colleagues establish, in Business & Information Systems Engineering in 2021, that if an organization is capable of accurately measuring its AI readiness and drawing the right conclusions before the adoption decision, it can reduce risk and improve the adoption decision.
79	
80	The evidence Damir Ilić and colleagues present in the Journal of UUNT Informatics and Computer Sciences in 2025 is that the introduction of chatbots led to a measurable reduction in customer service staffing, with an average decline of 17.8% across the sample, although qualitative findings clarify that these cutbacks do not equate to widespread job loss, while Anastassia Fedyk and colleagues, writing in the Review of Accounting Studies in 2022, report that AI adoption is associated with an increase in log audit fees per employee, significant at the 5% level. Writing in management revue in 2026, Samuel Will and colleagues show that analysis reveals shifts in five core work characteristics: autonomy becomes strategic detachment, task significance and task identity diminish, skill variety decreases, and feedback is mediated through metrics, reflecting a broader reconfiguration of business models in the platform economy wherein side hustlers transition from labor-intensive micro-entrepreneurs to orchestrators of automated systems.
81	
82	## Employment, Displacement, and Job Creation
83	
84	### Evidence for displacement at the occupational level
85	
86	### Evidence for reinstatement and new task creation
87	
88	These two findings jointly establish that reinstatement and new task creation are empirically observable phenomena across both historical industrial transformations and contemporary AI adoption, yet they cannot speak to each other's horizon: the historical evidence captures net task-content shifts over four decades at the industry level, while the cross-country regression reflects an economy-wide association that does not distinguish between genuine new-task creation and mere enhancement of existing roles. The juxtaposition of AI's capacity to generate new roles with the unresolved difficulty of pricing worker data implies that even as new-task creation proceeds, the institutional infrastructure for distributing reinstatement gains to the workers whose contributions enable it remains underdeveloped.
89	
90	The firm-level employment advantage must be bounded against evidence that platform-based work arrangements coexist with substantial labor-market inactivity and job-seeking, implying that new-task creation in digital contexts may accompany precarity rather than uniformly stable reinstatement.
91	
92	The juxtaposition of positive robot-adaptation effects on young entrants with heightened displacement risk perceptions among students reveals a generational fault line in how reinstatement opportunities are apprehended: those already in the labor market may capture new-task gains, while those still in training perceive primarily displacement risk, which in turn dampens their valuation of the very AI skills that would enable reinstatement, even as they broadly endorse retraining as necessary.
93	
94	The structural concentration of employment in the service sector intensifies the stakes of reinstatement, because the same AI systems that could displace large workforce shares also generate administrative efficiencies and predictive matching capabilities that reshape hiring—raising the unresolved question of whether firms will redeploy those efficiencies toward new-task creation or merely cost reduction, a tension the current evidence does not settle.
95	
96	### Why firm-level and aggregate estimates diverge
97	
98	The unit labels are declared metadata and are not fully stated in the quoted findings, so this is an indirect comparison of income across different units of analysis (occupation versus worker) and is not directly comparable. The unit labels are declared metadata and are not fully stated in the quoted findings, so this is an indirect comparison of income across different units of analysis (economy versus firm) and is not directly comparable. The unit labels are declared metadata and are not fully stated in the quoted findings, so this is an indirect comparison of employment across different units of analysis (worker versus economy) and is not directly comparable. The unit labels are declared metadata and are not fully stated in the quoted findings, so this is an indirect comparison of employment across different units of analysis (economy versus worker) and is not directly comparable.
99	
100	## Wages, Skills, and the Labor Share
101	
102	### Skill demand is being recomposed rather than simply raised
103	
104	Ludovic Dioszegi, writing in the AGORA INTERNATIONAL JOURNAL OF ECONOMICAL SCIENCES in 2026, report that engineering roles such as MLOps, Infrastructure and Enterprise, related to AI integration and scale, demand a broader technical skill set (mean >15), highlighting the value placed on versatility in these roles, while Data Scientist and AI/ML Engineer positions have an average number of required skills of 14. A 2026 AGORA INTERNATIONAL JOURNAL OF ECONOMICAL SCIENCES study by Ludovic Dioszegi finds that data Scientist and AI/ML Engineer positions have an average number of required skills of 14.1 and 14.0, respectively, suggesting that these roles prioritize specialized expertise in a few key areas rather than a broad range of skills. Ekaterina Zubkova establish, in the Journal of Digital Art & Humanities in 2026, that for roles involving customized product design, spatial reasoning frequently represents a more important predictor of long-term success than immediate proficiency in specific software applications.
105	
106	The evidence to this point is measured at the occupation level; what follows is measured at the region level. The evidence He Ying present in the Journal of Artificial Intelligence and Information in 2026 is that empirical work that aggregates micro-level data on high-, middle-, and low-skilled labor to the regional level finds a significantly positive association between industrial digitalization and the regional employment share of middle- and high-skilled labor, lifting the region's overall employment skill structure. Writing in the Journal of UUNT Informatics and Computer Sciences in 2025, Damir Ilić and colleagues show that the literature warns of double effects, as chatbots do replace lower-level jobs (up to a 20%) and transform agent roles towards strategic tasks, increasing productivity by 80%. He Ying, writing in the Journal of Artificial Intelligence and Information in 2026, report that a contracting, aging labor supply and a digitally transformed pattern of demand together raise the premium on scarce high-level skills, reinforcing value differentiation.
107	
108	A 2026 AGORA INTERNATIONAL JOURNAL OF ECONOMICAL SCIENCES study by Tsotne Zhghenti and Natia Khukhunaishvili finds that from postings published during January 2026 on major Georgian hiring platforms, only advertisements explicitly mentioning GenAI-related tasks, tools, or skills were selected, yielding 88 applicable postings retained for full coding.
109	
110	The evidence above is observational evidence; the evidence below is review evidence. Romana Emilia Cramarenco and colleagues establish, in Oeconomia Copernicana in 2023, that out of 15 selected articles, 11 focused on the need for reskilling as an adaptive behavior related to AI adoption, while only four articles addressed the need for upskilling. The evidence Wladyslaw Wornalkewicz present in Professional Pedagogics in 2026 is that the results confirm the ambivalent nature of artificial intelligence: on the one hand, it creates challenges for traditional employment structures, while on the other, it opens new opportunities for improving quality of life, enhancing the efficiency of urban systems, and supporting sustainable development.
111	
112	## Sectoral Disruption Across Industries
113	
114	### Manufacturing and the robotics evidence
115	
116	Writing in the International Journal of Apllied Mathematics in 2025, Siddhi Shaji show that the incorporation of AI-powered automation in sectors like manufacturing, retail, agriculture and textiles can displace billions of workers, most of whom lack the required skills for adapting to AI-enabled job opportunities, while Damioli and colleagues find, in their 2021 Eurasian Business Review paper, that the final dataset covers 5257 firms active in AI patenting for the years 2000–2016, with worldwide coverage and including firms belonging to manufacturing and service sectors. The displacement projection and the patenting base jointly establish the scale of potential restructuring but do not settle its pace, nor do they distinguish between displacement that materializes as unemployment and displacement that manifests as within-firm task reassignment.
117	
118	The evidence above is drawn at the firm level; the evidence below is drawn at the industry level. Oxana DENISSOVA and colleagues, writing in Applied Computer Science in 2025, report that results demonstrate consistent superiority of the Hybrid model across all three industries: Metallurgy (MAE = 33.4 persons, RMSE = 58.3, MAPE = 0.32%), Power Supply (MAE = 18.7 persons, RMSE = 28.8, MAPE = 0.18%), and Construction (MAE = 98.6 persons, RMSE = 169.3, MAPE = 0.39%).
119	
120	The displacement literature identifies skill gaps as the binding constraint on adaptation but draws an implication it does not test: the feelings of inadequacy and isolation that accompany evolving skill requirements in manufacturing may constitute a psychological barrier to retraining independent of the objective skill deficit, a channel entirely absent from the displacement projections. The industry-level forecasting precision cannot be validated against the available worker-level survey evidence, which is limited to short-tenure office and retail environments where most participants had fewer than four years of experience; this methodological mismatch leaves manufacturing-specific worker adjustment dynamics unidentified.
121	
122	### Professional, financial, and knowledge services
123	
124	The marginal employment growth associated with AI diffusion does not resolve which sectors absorb the offsetting negative trend, and the designation of financial services as among the most disrupted implies that the aggregate positive effect may mask concentrated sectoral displacement.
125	
126	A 2024 Arthshastra Indian Journal of Economics & Research study by Rangapriya Saivasan and Madhavi Lokhande finds that leveraging AI can improve healthcare outcomes, decrease gender pay inequalities, increase women's representation in male-dominated industries, and give women better access to financial services, while in the Inspire Intelligence Journal in 2025, Bhanu Ranjan and colleagues show that evolving skill requirements across sectors including healthcare, manufacturing, and financial services are undergoing substantial transformations influenced by AI and automation, engendering feelings of inadequacy and isolation among employees. Mohit Panwar establish, in the International Journal of Emerging Research in Engineering and Technology in 2026, that generative AI significantly enhances frontline efficiency by enabling real-time knowledge retrieval, automated reporting, natural language interaction, and context-aware recommendations, with GenAI adoption associated with reductions. These findings jointly establish that efficiency gains and expanded access to financial and healthcare services coexist with psychological costs for workers undergoing skill transformation, but the evidence does not settle whether the net welfare effect is positive once adaptation costs are internalized.
127	
128	The contrast between activity-level restructuring in knowledge services and the quantitative predictability of labor demand in heavy industries underscores that professional and financial services face a governance challenge distinct from sectoral forecasting: ensuring equitable AI adoption while the very unit of work is being redefined.
129	
130	### Healthcare, education, and the caring professions
131	
132	The evidence here is measured at the task level. The evidence Aman Kumar and colleagues present in the JOURNAL GLOBAL VALUES in 2023 is that artificial intelligence plays a significant role in the healthcare sector by aiding in the management of healthcare services, facilitating early diagnosis, enhancing documentation processes, and enabling robotic surgery. Writing in PNAS nexus in 2024, Ali Akbar Septiandri and colleagues show that cardiovascular Technologists and Technicians rank first among the most-impacted occupations based on AII scores, with 16 impacted tasks out of 25 total and an AII of 0.64. Joel Jia Wei Ng and colleagues, writing in BMC Medical Informatics and Decision Making in 2025, report that using speech recognition for clinical documentation was significantly slower, with task completion times being 18.11% longer compared to using keyboard and mouse, and resulted in 390 errors compared to 245 in KBM, including errors with potential patient harm, while Shreya J Shah and colleagues find, in their 2025 JAMA Network Open paper, that the 33 comments about accuracy were largely negative, with 5 positive comments (15%) and 28 negative comments (85%). A 2022 Cell Reports Medicine study by Enrico Coiera and Sidong Liu finds that pre-pandemic, this lag led to significant unnecessary waste across the healthcare system of up to $274 billion per year globally. Tiffany Brooks and colleagues establish, in the Journal of Pain Research in 2020, that cPP affects approximately 15% of women worldwide and has significant psychological, physical and financial impact on the lives of sufferers, and in NAM Perspectives in 2022, Sergio Aguilar-Gaxiola and colleagues show that a conceptual model should consider that health is not just about being free of disease or infirmity, but that individuals and communities have the right to thrive—to reach the enjoyment of the highest attainable standard of health. The prevalence of chronic pelvic pain and the emphasis on thriving rather than merely disease-free status suggest that caring professions involve relational and psychosocial dimensions that task-level AI impact metrics do not capture, limiting the applicability of patent-similarity scores to occupations whose core value lies in therapeutic presence.
133	
134	The evidence above is drawn at the task level; the evidence below is drawn at the economy level. The evidence Aman Kumar and colleagues present in the JOURNAL GLOBAL VALUES in 2023 is that AI in the realm of education offers sophisticated instructional methodologies to students through the utilization of data mining techniques, intelligent teaching platforms, and personalized learning systems. Writing in the Interdisciplinary Journal of Innovation in Nepalese Academia in 2025, Sagar Bishwakarma and Smeeth Bista show that a significant majority (77.17%) are studying in public higher education institutions, while Silah Hayati Kamsani and Darren Maing Anak Abitt, writing in the International Journal of Research and Innovation in Social Science in 2025, report that the QR code-based system demonstrated low implementation costs (RM 768.25/USD 62 per laboratory station) using standard office equipment and basic camera modules, making automation education accessible within typical Malaysian institutional budgets. Felix Aberu and Tersia Botha, writing in Acta Academiae Beregsasiensis Economics in 2026, report that unemployment among individuals with advanced education exhibits a strong negative effect on sustainable development in the short run, with a coefficient of −1.025 (p = 0.027), implying that a 1% increase in unemployment among highly educated workers reduces sustainable development by approximately 1.02%.
135	
136	The evidence to this point is measured at the economy level; what follows is measured at the worker level. A 2024 Artificial Intelligence Review study by Francisco Bolaños and colleagues finds that only eight of the evaluated tools implement at least 70% of the designated features, with DistillerSR, Nested Knowledge, Dextr, and ExaCT leading with the highest feature coverage at 82%, while Matthew J Page and colleagues find, in their 2021 BMJ paper, that this approach can increase review efficiency by enabling the review team to start on subsequent steps of the review while less relevant records are still being screened. Mauro Cazzaniga and colleagues establish, in Economía in 2025, that the negative impact is more pronounced for Brazilian workers compared to those in the UK, with college-educated individuals facing a –27.9 percent vs. –24.1 percent impact, and non-college-educated workers seeing a –31.7 percent vs. –28.5 percent impact, respectively. The evidence Yolanda González-Castro and colleagues present in the Journal of Infrastructure Policy and Development in 2025 is that a total of 367 unique documents were identified in both WoS and Scopus, underscoring the depth and breadth of research in this area. The volume of identified research on new-age professions bounds the scope of existing evidence; while growing, it remains insufficient to resolve questions about long-run occupational transformation in healthcare and education, where task-level displacement metrics and tool-feature evaluations capture only fragments of a larger restructuring process.
137	
138	### Creative work and the generative-AI frontier
139	
140	Writing in the Columbia Journal of Tax Law in 2025, Jeremy Bearer-Friend and Sarah Polcz show that law, medicine, and creative industries are set to be among the first fields impacted, while Leonardo Banh and Gero Strobel find, in their 2023 Electronic Markets paper, that generative AI finds its utility across various modalities, including the generation of text, image, video, code, sound, and other produced content, such as molecules or 3D renderings. Thomas Montefiore and colleagues, writing in the Journal of Business Ethics in 2026, report that according to 2011 Australian census data, creative industries with very high creative intensity, such as motion picture and video production (76% creative intensity), will likely have more creative work impacted by AI than creative industries with lower creative intensity, such as museum operations (27% creative intensity), while Jainendra Kumar Verma and Kamal Krishna, writing in the International Journal of Foreign Trade and International Business in 2026, report that generative AI redesigns work even without formal redesign: it shifts the locus of human contribution from production to specifying, evaluating, and correcting machine output, raising the premium on judgment just as it lowers the premium on routine cognitive execution.
141	
142	The overreliance evidence bounds the judgment-premium thesis advanced in the work-redesign literature: if sustained delegation to generative systems erodes the very decision-making capacities that the redesign reallocates toward human judgment, the productivity gains documented in early experiments may not translate into durable capability expansion for workers in creative fields. The sectoral-intensity evidence remains limited by its inattention to within-culture and across-culture factors that prescribe how work and home roles are shared between the sexes: the gendered and cultural dimensions of who enters creative occupations, and who bears the consequences of AI disruption within them, are unresolved by the current evidence.
143	
144	## Critical Synthesis: What the Literature Establishes and What It Does Not
145	
146	### What the evidence establishes
147	
148	Regional displacement metrics similarly diverge from firm- and worker-level outcomes, establishing that spatial aggregates and individual labor experiences capture distinct dimensions of employment restructuring. A 2023 npj Urban Sustainability study by Roberta Capello and Camilla Lenzi finds that the regional marginal effect of robot density on employment share for the top 25% of cities is 0.003, while in İnsan ve Toplum Bilimleri Araştırmaları Dergisi in 2025, Mustafa Ergün show that worker-level informal employment declined only moderately from 27.6% in 2019 to 19.2% in 2023. The unit labels are declared metadata and are not fully stated in the quoted findings, so this is an indirect comparison of employment across different units of analysis (economy versus firm) and is not directly comparable. The unit labels are declared metadata and are not fully stated in the quoted findings, so this is an indirect comparison of employment across different units of analysis (economy versus industry) and is not directly comparable. The unit labels are declared metadata and are not fully stated in the quoted findings, so this is an indirect comparison of employment across different units of analysis (region versus worker) and is not directly comparable. The unit labels are declared metadata and are not fully stated in the quoted findings, so this is an indirect comparison of employment across different units of analysis (region versus firm) and is not directly comparable. These employment findings are not contradictory: the two estimates observe different units of analysis, and the evidence establishes the effect at the region level without establishing it at the industry level. What the evidence does not settle is the magnitude: the estimates on employment are not on the same footing, and the literature cannot distinguish a real difference from an artefact of how each was measured.
149	
150	### Where the literature genuinely disagrees
151	
152	The unit labels are declared metadata and are not fully stated in the quoted findings, so this is an indirect comparison of employment across different units of analysis (occupation versus worker) and is not directly comparable. The unit labels are declared metadata and are not fully stated in the quoted findings, so this is an indirect comparison of employment across different units of analysis (occupation versus firm) and is not directly comparable. The unit labels are declared metadata and are not fully stated in the quoted findings, so this is an indirect comparison of employment across different units of analysis (occupation versus industry) and is not directly comparable. These earnings findings are not contradictory: the two estimates observe different units of analysis, and the evidence establishes the effect at the worker level without establishing it at the occupation level. The unit labels are declared metadata and are not fully stated in the quoted findings, so this is an indirect comparison of earnings across different units of analysis (worker versus firm) and is not directly comparable. These income findings are not contradictory in studies of occupations: the two estimates observe different units of analysis, and the evidence establishes the effect at the occupation level without establishing it at the economy level.
153	
154	### What the evidence cannot yet resolve
155	
156	The literature on AI's restructuring of employment cannot yet resolve whether the magnitude of displacement observed at one unit of analysis generalizes to another, because region-, firm-, industry-, and worker-level studies employ fundamentally different identification strategies and outcome measures that are not commensurable. Roberta Capello and Camilla Lenzi establish, in npj Urban Sustainability in 2023, that robot density is associated with a −0.005*** decline in the employment-to-population ratio for top 25% cities, while robot density growth is associated with a −0.109** decline for the same group, while Nicolae Istudor and colleagues find, in their 2025 Journal of Business Economics and Management paper, that the perception that acquired personal AI skills influence overall AI impact is moderated by the risk of not finding a job because of AI (β = –0.167, t-value = 3.665, p < 0.001). The evidence Roberta Capello and Camilla Lenzi present in npj Urban Sustainability in 2023 is that robot density growth is associated with a −0.136*** decline in employment to population ratio for non-city areas at the top 10% city grouping, while Musa Nouman Siddiqui and Dr Nasir Khan, writing in the ACADEMIA International Journal for Social Sciences in 2025, report that AI-driven news creation technologies have led to some organizations reporting up to a 50% reduction in employment expenditures.
157	
158	## Implications and a Research Agenda
159	
160	### Implications that follow from the mechanisms
161	
162	Writing in the London journal of interdisciplinary sciences in 2023, Adam Sharif and colleagues show that jobs susceptible to full AI takeover make up 6.3% of the labor force, and if AI fully takes over, millions of jobs will be lost, bringing job instability. Hasraddin Guliyev, writing in Research in Globalization in 2023, report that the unemployment rate reduces by 0.003 percent as the search volume related to artificial intelligence increases in 24 high-tech developed countries.
163	
164	The evidence above is drawn at the occupation level; the evidence below is drawn at the industry level. A 2025 Applied Computer Science study by Oxana DENISSOVA and colleagues finds that the transformed production volume variable has the strongest influence, accounting for approximately 58.3% of the total predictive power, highlighting the scale effect associated with production expansion and its direct impact on employment dynamics. Atullya Tripathi establish, in the Alliance Journal of Corporate and Commercial Law in 2025, that AI algorithms are educated on data; if that data represents current society prejudices, the AI systems will reinforce and even amplify these prejudices, and discriminatory results in recruiting, promotions, and other HR procedures may follow from this, while in Archives for Technical Sciences in 2025, E Pavithra show that AI-based recruitment, performance measurement, and decision-support systems have a tremendous positive influence on operational efficiency, employee productivity, and strategic alignment. The evidence Adeyemi Olatunbosun and colleagues present in the Journal of economics, business and commerce in 2025 is that social protection mechanisms—unemployment benefits, job criteria, and wage stabilizations—are required to support the workers who will be removed by automation and assist them in adapting to new jobs. Writing in Izzivi prihodnosti in 2025, Alexander van Biezen show that at the societal level, the research underlines the urgency of open policy debates and ethical reflection on AI regulation, and addressing the implications of AI autonomy is essential to preserve human agency, democratic accountability, and social justice in the digital era.
165	
166	The research agenda that follows from these mechanisms is constrained by where evidence has been collected, how it has been measured, and whether the relational architecture of workplaces—now shown to moderate performance and well-being outcomes—has been adequately integrated into models of AI's restructuring effects. Trinh Nguyen and Amany Elbanna, writing in Information Systems Frontiers in 2025, report that further longitudinal and case study research is needed to explore the nuances of how AI automation and AI augmentation occur in practice and the implications this may have for workers and organisations. The calls for longitudinal designs, primary workplace data, and methodologically rigorous observation jointly establish that the field cannot yet adjudicate whether AI augmentation mitigates or exacerbates within-occupation inequality, because existing evidence has neither tracked the same workers through actual transitions nor measured workplace conditions at sufficient resolution to distinguish precarious from higher-quality employment outcomes.
167	
168	### Gaps the evidence itself exposes
169	
170	The unit labels are declared metadata and are not fully stated in the quoted findings, so this is an indirect comparison of job loss across different units of analysis (worker versus industry) and is not directly comparable. The unit labels are declared metadata and are not fully stated in the quoted findings, so this is an indirect comparison of job loss across different units of analysis (firm versus industry) and is not directly comparable. The unit labels are declared metadata and are not fully stated in the quoted findings, so this is an indirect comparison of employment across different units of analysis (industry versus firm) and is not directly comparable. The productivity finding in studies of employment rests on a observational design in one case and a quasi-experimental design in the other; the evidence therefore establishes it more securely than either study alone. The efficiency finding in studies of improve rests on a survey design in one case and a quasi-experimental design in the other; the evidence therefore establishes it more securely than either study alone. The unit labels are declared metadata and are not fully stated in the quoted findings, so this is an indirect comparison of efficiency across different units of analysis (firm versus worker) and is not directly comparable.
